title: Sharia
summary: In Muslim thought, sharia is God's eternal way for human life, known in full only to God; what people call sharia in practice is fiqh, the centuries of fallible human jurisprudence that tries to understand and derive it. Plural, revisable, and overwhelmingly concerned with worship and everyday conduct rather than the harsh punishments of Western caricature, it is drawn from the Quran and the Prophet's example through schools of law that disagree among themselves — and its meaning is fiercely contested in the modern age of nation-states.
hook: Ask what sharia says about almost anything, and the honest answer begins with a distinction most people never hear. Sharia is the divine ideal: God's path for how to live, complete and unchanging, but known in full only to God. What human beings actually possess is fiqh — the long, fallible labor of scholars trying to understand that path and turn it into rules. So every concrete ruling, judgment, or fatwa ever called 'sharia' has passed through human interpretation — plural, argued-over, and revisable, not a single fixed code handed down intact. The schools of law disagree with one another, often sharply, on what God requires. Most of what they argue about is prayer, fasting, marriage, charity, and trade. The harsh criminal punishments that fill the Western imagination are a small, hedged-about corner of a vast tradition that is mostly about how to worship and how to be decent.

plainEnglish.theConcept:
People say "sharia says" this or that. They talk as if sharia were one simple rulebook anyone could open and read.

It is not. In Muslim belief, sharia is God's way for human life. It is the path God wants people to walk. But that path is complete and perfect. Only God fully knows it.

What people actually have is something more human. Scholars spent centuries studying the holy sources. They tried to work out what God requires. That human effort, the actual rules and reasoning, has its own name. It is called fiqh.

This difference matters more than almost anything else about the subject. Sharia is the divine ideal. Fiqh is the human attempt to understand it. So when people speak of sharia in courts or in daily choices, they are really talking about fiqh. And fiqh was made by people who often disagreed.

Because people made it, it comes in many versions. There is no single office that speaks for God. So "sharia says" is almost always too simple a thing to say.
plainEnglish.problemItAddresses:
Every community needs to answer a basic question. How should we live? What does God want from us, day by day?

For Muslims, the answer starts with two sources. The first is the Quran, the holy book Muslims believe to be God's own words. The second is the example of the Prophet Muhammad. This is called the sunna, and it is remembered in short reports.

But these sources do not answer every question directly. The Quran tells believers to pray. It does not always spell out exactly how. New situations come up that no text mentions by name.

So scholars needed a method. When the texts were silent, they used careful comparison. Wine is forbidden because it clouds the mind. So what about a new drink that does the same thing?

This is the real work of Islamic law. It is not looking up a fixed rule. It is reasoning, carefully and humbly, toward what God most likely wants.
plainEnglish.competingTraditions:
Here is a fact that surprises many people. There has never been just one Islamic law. There have been several schools, and they disagree.

Among Sunni Muslims, the largest branch, four schools survived. They are named after early teachers. They are called Hanafi, Maliki, Shafi'i, and Hanbali. Shia Muslims are a different branch. They have their own school, often called the Jafari school.

These schools are not rivals at war. They respect one another. A Muslim usually follows the school common in their region. But the schools reach different rulings on real questions. How should a marriage contract be written? How is an inheritance divided? On many such points, the schools simply differ. One allows what another discourages. Each can give reasons rooted in the sources.

This is the heart of the matter. Thoughtful people read the same sources and reached different conclusions. So many questions carry more than one answer that the tradition accepts as defensible. The disagreement is not a flaw. It is built into a tradition that knows it is human work.
plainEnglish.politicalStakes:
Most of Islamic law has nothing to do with crime or courts. It covers how to wash before prayer, how to fast in Ramadan, how to give to the poor, and what foods are allowed.

Scholars sorted human actions onto a five-step scale. An act can be required, recommended, neutral, discouraged, or forbidden. Most of daily life falls in the middle, as advice rather than command.

The harsh punishments that fill Western news are real and severe, but rare. They are called the hudud, a small set of fixed penalties named in scripture. Classical scholars hedged them with so many conditions. They demanded such near-impossible proof that these penalties were meant to stay rare.

The modern fight is about something else, the state. For most of history, Islamic law was the work of scholars more than of governments, though rulers appointed the judges and enforced the verdicts. Today some countries write parts of it into national codes. Some apply only family law. A few apply criminal law. Political movements demand "the sharia" as a slogan. These are modern choices, and Muslims argue about them.
plainEnglish.contestedHistoryC:
Sharia can feel timeless, as if it dropped from heaven complete. The reality is a long human story with a starting point.

The Prophet Muhammad died in the year 632. While he lived, hard questions could be brought to him. After his death, that direct guidance was gone. The community had the Quran and its memories of him. But it had to learn how to apply them on its own.

The great schools of law did not exist yet. They took shape slowly, over the next two or three centuries. One early teacher, al-Shafi'i, helped set out how the sources should rank and connect. By around the tenth century, the main schools had crystallized.

So the law that feels ancient was actually built, piece by piece, by generations of arguing scholars. The law was never frozen. It grew, branched, and was revised, because the people making it knew they might be wrong.
plainEnglish.whereDebateStands:
Today the word sharia carries a heavy charge in the West. It is often imagined as nothing but stoning and severed hands. That caricature takes the rarest, most extreme corner of the law and treats it as the whole.

The real debates among Muslims are wide and serious. Should old rulings on women, on banking, on minorities be rethought for modern life? Many scholars say the tradition has always allowed fresh reasoning. Others warn against bending the law to fit the times.

There is also the question of the state. When a government enforces its version of sharia, whose version is it? A human committee chooses, then calls its choice God's law. Critics inside Islam find that troubling.

And for many Muslims living as minorities, sharia is mainly private guidance. It guides worship, food, and family, within their country's law. They are not asking to replace national law.

Sharia is a living, plural, human tradition. It is still being worked out.

general.theConcept:
In English, people often say "sharia law." The phrase is natural, but it can flatten an important distinction. In the tradition's own terms it nearly repeats itself, and it hides the distinction that organizes the entire subject. Sharia, in classical Muslim thought, is the divine way: God's complete and perfect path for human life, known in its fullness only to God. It is an ideal, not a book on a shelf.

What scholars and judges actually handle is fiqh, the human science of understanding that ideal and deriving rules from it. The Arabic word means, roughly, deep understanding. Fiqh is by definition fallible, because it is the product of human reasoning about sacred sources. To speak of sharia as a fixed code that "says" something is to collapse the very gap the tradition insists on.

This is not a modern apology designed to soften a harsh subject. It is the classical self-understanding. Scholars routinely closed their legal opinions with the formula that God knows best. They were claiming to approximate the divine will, not to possess it. The consequence is decisive: every actual ruling carries the fingerprint of a human interpreter, and could in principle have come out otherwise.
general.problemItAddresses:
The problem sharia addresses is the problem of guidance after revelation ends. Muslims hold that God spoke through the Quran and that the Prophet's life showed how to live by it. But the Prophet died in 632, and revelation closed with him, while the questions did not stop coming.

The answer was a method, gathered under the name usul al-fiqh, the roots or sources of the law. Four are classical for Sunnis. The Quran comes first. Then the sunna, the Prophet's example transmitted through hadith, the second great source, which supplies the operative detail scripture leaves general. Then ijma, the consensus of qualified scholars. Then qiyas, reasoning by analogy from a known ruling to a new case.

Around these sit further tools. Ijtihad is the independent reasoning a qualified jurist exercises when the sources do not yield a direct answer. Maslaha weighs the public interest, the welfare a ruling serves. Different schools rank and trust these instruments differently, and that is one reason they reach different conclusions. The method is not a machine that prints a single output. It is a disciplined argument with room for disagreement built in.
general.competingTraditions:
When people say "Islam teaches" some specific rule, a scholar's first instinct is to ask which school. Sunni law preserved four that still function: the Hanafi, widespread from Turkey through South Asia; the Maliki, dominant across North and West Africa; the Shafi'i, strong in Egypt and Southeast Asia; and the Hanbali, associated with the Arabian Peninsula. Shia Islam developed its own jurisprudence, the Jafari school chief among them, which grants special weight to teachings transmitted through the Prophet's family.

These schools differ in method and in countless particular rulings. The Hanafis lean more readily on reasoned analogy and the jurist's judgment; some others stay closer to the explicit reports. The practical results diverge on marriage, divorce, inheritance, ritual, and commerce. An act treated as merely discouraged in one school may be flatly forbidden in another.

The schools regarded this plurality as legitimate. A famous sentiment in the tradition held that disagreement among the scholars was a mercy, not a scandal, because it widened the room for sound practice. This internal pluralism is the strongest correction to the idea that sharia speaks with one voice. The same sources, read by serious and pious people, have always produced more than one defensible answer.
general.politicalStakes:
The Western imagination fixes on the hudud, the small set of fixed scriptural penalties for offenses such as theft and adultery. They are real, and they are severe. But within classical fiqh they form a narrow category, fenced by evidentiary demands so high that conviction was meant to be exceptional rather than routine. To convict of adultery by the strict standard, for instance, required four eyewitnesses to the act itself, a near-impossible bar.

The overwhelming bulk of the law lies elsewhere. Jurists divided it broadly into ibadat, the rules of worship, and muamalat, the rules of dealings between people. Worship covers prayer, fasting, charity, and the pilgrimage. Dealings cover contracts, marriage, inheritance, and trade, including the much-discussed ban on riba, usually understood as interest. Every act gets placed on a five-fold scale running from obligatory through recommended, neutral, and discouraged, to forbidden.

What changed in the modern era was the relationship between law and state. Classical fiqh was a scholars' law, developed in seminaries and applied by jurists with real independence from rulers. The modern nation-state has pulled that law into its own machinery, and the political fights that follow turn on who now holds the authority the scholars once held.
general.contestedHistoryC:
The standard picture of a law revealed whole and frozen does not survive contact with its history. What the Prophet left at his death in 632 was a community, a scripture, and a remembered example, not a finished legal system. The system was built afterward, and the building took centuries.

For several generations, legal practice was relatively fluid, shaped by local custom and the judgment of early authorities. The turn toward a formal theory of sources is conventionally associated with al-Shafi'i in the early ninth century, who argued for disciplining legal reasoning by anchoring it firmly in the Quran and the authenticated sunna. The major schools took on their lasting shape across the ninth and tenth centuries.

Historians have argued at length about how this happened. An older view, associated with the scholar Joseph Schacht, held that much early legal material was projected backward onto the Prophet to lend it authority, implying the law owed more to later practice than to revelation. Later scholars such as Wael Hallaq have stressed instead the gradual, organic growth of a genuine jurists' tradition. The dispute is real, but both sides agree on the essential point: sharia as a worked-out body of law is a human historical achievement, not a single moment of disclosure.
general.whereDebateStands:
The modern history of sharia is, above all, the story of its collision with the nation-state, and that collision fractured a tradition that had run for a thousand years as a scholars' law. Across the nineteenth and twentieth centuries, colonial administrations and reforming governments codified Islamic rules into statutes, transplanted European legal codes alongside or over them, and moved authority from independent jurists to legislatures and bureaucracies. The result is the spectrum visible today.

At one end are largely secular states that retain sharia-influenced family law while governing most else by civil code, a pattern common across the Muslim world. In the middle are states that apply a broader Islamic criminal law. At a third position stand Islamist movements that demand the implementation of "the sharia" as a political program, often treating it as a fixed code in exactly the way the classical tradition did not. Each of these is a modern arrangement, and Muslims disagree sharply about all of them.

Two further debates run underneath. One concerns reform: whether the tradition's own tool of independent reasoning, ijtihad, can responsibly rework inherited rulings on questions such as gender and finance, and how far is too far. The other concerns Muslims living as minorities, for whom sharia operates mostly as private religious guidance rather than enforceable law, a reality frequently distorted in the politics of countries where they are a small share of the population. None of these questions has a settled answer, and the tradition's plural, human character is precisely what keeps them open.

advanced.theConcept:
The analytically load-bearing claim is that sharia and fiqh name two different things that ordinary usage conflates, and that nearly every confusion about Islamic law dissolves once the pair is kept distinct. Sharia denotes the divine norm in itself, exhaustively known only to God; fiqh denotes the epistemic enterprise of apprehending it, irreducibly probabilistic in its results. Classical legal theory was unusually explicit that most fiqh yields zann, considered probability, rather than certainty, and that a qualified jurist who reasons soundly is rewarded even when wrong.

This built a principled fallibilism into the system's foundations. The doctrine that every competent mujtahid is, in some accounts, correct, or at least not culpably mistaken, licensed precisely the plurality of schools that a code-model of law cannot accommodate. It also frames the modern crisis. When a state promulgates a statute and labels it sharia, it converts probable human interpretation into fixed positive command, erasing the gap between the divine norm and its human apprehension that the tradition was built to preserve. The category error is not incidental to contemporary disputes; it is their engine.
advanced.problemItAddresses:
Usul al-fiqh, the theory of the sources, is best read not as a discovery procedure that mechanically generates rulings but as a structure that distributes and constrains interpretive authority. Its four classical Sunni roots, Quran, sunna, ijma, and qiyas, do not rank as a simple lexical hierarchy; the relations among them, and the status of supplementary principles such as maslaha, istihsan, and custom, were themselves contested across the schools. The discipline's real work was to specify who could reason, from what, under which controls.

The pivotal and still-debated question concerns ijtihad and its supposed closure. A widely repeated thesis held that the "gate of ijtihad" shut around the tenth century, after which jurists merely followed precedent within their schools. Wael Hallaq's influential intervention argued that the gate never closed, that creative legal reasoning persisted under other names, and that the closure narrative was retrospective myth. The stakes are immediate: if independent reasoning was always available, then modern reformist ijtihad is continuous with the tradition rather than a rupture from it, and the burden shifts to traditionalists to explain why renewed reasoning should now be resisted.
advanced.competingTraditions:
Treating the madhhabs as four interchangeable variants understates what divided them. They rested on partly different theories of evidence and of the very sources, the Hanafi tradition's deployment of istihsan, juristic preference, against a stricter textualism elsewhere; the Maliki appeal to the practice of Medina as itself probative; the divergent valuations of solitary hadith reports. These were disagreements about epistemology, not merely about outcomes, and they generated incompatible accounts of how a ruling earns its authority.

The Sunni-Shia divergence cuts deeper still, because it relocates the source of post-prophetic authority. Jafari jurisprudence grounds the law in transmission through the Imams of the Prophet's family and, in its dominant Usuli form, sustains a living hierarchy of jurists whose considered reasoning binds their followers, a structure with no Sunni equivalent. The confronting implication is that there is no neutral, supra-school vantage from which to pronounce what "sharia says." Authority is always certified by a particular community's account of who may be trusted to carry and interpret the sources, the same problem that governs which hadith each tradition will even admit. The plurality is not surface variation atop a shared core; it goes to the root of how the divine will is known at all.
advanced.politicalStakes:
The hudud occupy a place in analysis wildly disproportionate to their place in the law, and the disproportion is itself the datum worth examining. As a juristic category they are few, textually fixed, and hedged by evidentiary and procedural conditions, the demand for multiple eyewitnesses, the doctrine that doubt averts the penalty, that classical jurists elaborated in ways that systematically narrowed their application. Their modern prominence owes less to classical fiqh than to a political theater of authenticity in which states and movements perform Islamic governance through its most visible and punitive symbols.

The deeper transformation is institutional. Wael Hallaq's strong thesis holds that the modern state is not a neutral vessel into which sharia can be poured but a structure incompatible with it, because classical Islamic law presupposed a community of jurists operating with moral and epistemic independence from political power, a separation the centralizing, codifying, sovereign state abolishes. On this reading, "Islamic state" verges on contradiction: the state's monopoly over law destroys the very jurists' authority that made the law sharia rather than royal decree. Critics counter that Hallaq romanticizes a premodern autonomy that was always entangled with power, and that codified Islamic law, whatever its compromises, is a legitimate adaptation. The argument is unresolved, and a great deal of contemporary Muslim political life is lived inside it.
advanced.contestedHistoryC:
Reconstructing the law's first century and a half remains the hardest problem in the field, because the earliest systematic legal texts postdate the events they describe and rest on chains of transmission whose reliability is exactly what is in question. Schacht's account inferred from the back-growth of legal isnads that much attributed prophetic material was later doctrine projected onto earlier authority; the inference made the formative period look more like retrospective construction than faithful preservation. The methodological difficulty is that the tradition's own records are both the principal evidence and the thing under suspicion.

Subsequent scholarship complicated the picture in both directions. Work in the line of Harald Motzki applied isnad-cum-matn analysis to push some legal material earlier than Schacht allowed, while Hallaq and others emphasized the organic emergence of a jurists' culture over the conspiratorial overtones of pure back-projection. What cannot be evaded is that the consolidation of usul al-fiqh, conventionally pivoting on al-Shafi'i, was a normative achievement that imposed order on a more variegated earlier practice, and that the genealogy connecting that order to the Prophet's own time is precisely the contested terrain. The point with the highest stakes is that the authority of the whole edifice rests on a historical claim that the available evidence can neither fully secure nor fully refute.
advanced.whereDebateStands:
The contemporary condition is best described as the aftermath of a rupture rather than a continuation of the classical tradition. Codification, the nation-state, and colonial legal transplants did not merely add new rules; they dismantled the institutional ecology, the autonomous jurists, the schools as living communities of reasoning, the madrasa-trained authority, within which fiqh had functioned as fiqh. What survives often retains classical vocabulary while operating by a fundamentally different logic, that of state positive law.

This frames the sharpest current disagreements. Reformists, drawing on the openness of ijtihad and on principles such as maslaha and the higher objectives of the law, the maqasid, argue that the tradition's own resources authorize substantial revision on gender, finance, criminal penalties, and minority rights. Traditionalists reply that ungrounded reform untethered from the formal discipline collapses into reading present preferences into the sources, the same charge once leveled within the classical schools. Between them sits the politicized Western debate, in which "sharia" functions less as a legal-historical object than as a cipher for anxieties about Muslim populations, and in which the elementary distinction between a private religious ethic and an enforced state code is routinely effaced. The scholarly task is to hold these apart, to describe the spectrum of actual arrangements with precision, and to refuse the false choice between caricature and apologetics that the public argument keeps offering.

foundation.theStory: Sharia poses a problem of a particular kind: how a community translates a closed revelation into livable guidance for situations its scriptures never named, across many centuries and cultures. After the Prophet Muhammad died in 632, Muslims held the Quran and the memory of his example but no finished legal system. The story of sharia is the story of how scholars built one, through a science of interpretation called fiqh, and why that human science produced not a single code but several competing schools that endure to this day. The decisive move for any newcomer is to separate sharia, the divine ideal known fully only to God, from fiqh, the fallible human effort to grasp it.
foundation.theDebate: The central debates concern the relationship between the divine ideal and its human interpretation, the historical formation of the law in the first Islamic centuries, the legitimacy of the plurality of schools, and above all the fate of the tradition under the modern nation-state. Did the modern era reform Islamic law or break it? Can the tradition's own tool of independent reasoning, ijtihad, responsibly revise inherited rulings, or does that untether the law from its sources? These are live arguments among Muslims, not settled doctrines.
foundation.whyItMatters: Understanding sharia unlocks how Islamic law and ethics actually work, why the Quran alone does not determine them, and why "sharia says X" is almost always too simple. It illuminates the difference between Sunni and Shia communities, the place of the hadith as a source of law, and the politics of states and movements that invoke Islamic law today. It also dismantles a widespread caricature that reduces a vast tradition of worship and conduct to a handful of criminal punishments.
questions: Muslim scholars distinguish sharia, God's perfect and complete way known only to God, from fiqh, the human jurisprudence that tries to derive it. Why does this distinction matter so much, and what changes when a government writes its own interpretation into law and calls the result simply 'sharia'?

Islamic law preserved several schools that reach different rulings from the same sources, and the tradition often treated this disagreement as legitimate. What are the advantages of a legal tradition that builds plurality into its foundations, and what problems might that plurality create?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The conversation runs between scholars who reconstruct the law's formation and those who analyze its modern transformation. For the formative period, Joseph Schacht's mid-century thesis that much early legal material was retrospectively attributed to the Prophet set the terms, and was complicated by later work, including Wael Hallaq's account of an organic jurists' tradition and the gate of ijtihad that, he argued, never closed. For the modern condition, Hallaq's strong claim that the nation-state is structurally incompatible with classical sharia anchors one pole, while scholars such as Noah Feldman trace how Islamic constitutionalism actually balanced ruler and jurist. Reform-minded jurists such as Khaled Abou El Fadl and Mohammad Hashim Kamali argue from within the tradition for renewed reasoning.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidence includes the Quran and hadith corpora, the manuals of legal theory and the schools' substantive rulings, biographical and court records, and the modern statutes that codified Islamic rules. The limits are real. The earliest formative period is documented by sources that postdate it and whose transmission chains are themselves contested, so claims about origins carry irreducible uncertainty. And because fiqh is by nature plural, generalizations about what "the law" holds must always be indexed to a school, a place, and a period, or they mislead.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong argument must refuse two easy paths: the caricature that equates sharia with its harshest penalties, and the apologetic that presents it as seamlessly liberal. It might proceed by taking the sharia-fiqh distinction seriously and following its consequences, for instance, arguing that because actual rulings are admittedly probable human interpretation, the modern codifier's claim to enact God's law as fixed statute is in tension with the tradition's own epistemology. It must then specify what the historical evidence can support about the law's formation and what it cannot, and locate any normative claim within a particular school and context rather than a generic 'Islam.'
questions: Wael Hallaq argues that the modern centralizing state cannot house classical sharia, because that law depended on jurists who reasoned independently of political power. If he is right, what becomes of movements that demand an 'Islamic state' applying 'the sharia'? If he is wrong, what does a legitimate modern accommodation of Islamic law require?

Reformers argue that ijtihad, independent legal reasoning, authorizes substantial revision of inherited rulings; traditionalists warn that reform untethered from the formal discipline collapses into reading present preferences into the sources. What standards could distinguish a disciplined reinterpretation from an arbitrary one?

research.topic: The distinction between sharia and fiqh and the classical structure of the sources
research.content: It is well established within both the Islamic tradition and academic scholarship that classical thought distinguished sharia, the divine way known fully only to God, from fiqh, the human jurisprudence that derives rulings from it, and treated most fiqh as yielding probability (zann) rather than certainty. The Sunni theory of the sources (usul al-fiqh) canonically lists four roots: the Quran, the sunna (the Prophet's example transmitted via hadith), ijma (scholarly consensus), and qiyas (analogical reasoning), supplemented by contested principles such as maslaha (public interest) and istihsan (juristic preference). Four Sunni schools survived (Hanafi, Maliki, Shafi'i, Hanbali) alongside the Shia Jafari school, and the consolidation of a formal source theory is conventionally associated with al-Shafi'i (d. 820) in the early ninth century, with the major schools crystallizing across the ninth and tenth centuries. The law classifies acts on a five-fold scale from obligatory to forbidden and divides broadly into ibadat (worship) and muamalat (dealings).

research.topic: Whether the modern nation-state can house classical sharia
research.content: A significant recent scholarly argument, advanced most forcefully by Wael Hallaq in The Impossible State (2013), holds that the modern sovereign, centralizing nation-state is structurally incompatible with classical sharia, because Islamic law presupposed a community of jurists operating with moral and epistemic independence from political power, an independence the codifying state abolishes when it monopolizes legislation. On this reading, codified 'Islamic law' retains classical vocabulary while operating by the alien logic of state positive law, and the notion of an 'Islamic state' approaches contradiction. The thesis is contested: critics argue that Hallaq idealizes a premodern juristic autonomy that was in practice entangled with rulers, and that codification, whatever its compromises, is a legitimate adaptation rather than a betrayal. Scholars including Noah Feldman have traced how premodern Islamic governance balanced ruler and jurist, complicating any sharp before-and-after narrative. The debate is active and bears directly on contemporary Islamist politics.

research.topic: The precise content and authority of legal practice in the first Islamic century
research.content: What evidence would resolve this: contemporaneous legal documents, court records, or systematic juristic writings from the decades immediately following the Prophet's death in 632, against which the later tradition's account of early rulings and their prophetic warrant could be checked. Why that evidence is unavailable: the earliest comprehensive legal and theoretical texts postdate the formative period by roughly one to two centuries; legal transmission in that interval was substantially oral and is recorded through chains (isnads) whose reliability is itself the central object of dispute; and there are few sources external to the tradition against which those chains can be independently verified. What can be responsibly inferred: that early practice was relatively fluid and shaped by local custom and the judgment of early authorities; that a formal theory of sources consolidated around al-Shafi'i and after; and, via isnad-cum-matn analysis in the line of Harald Motzki, that some specific legal traditions were in circulation earlier than the strongest skeptics (notably Joseph Schacht) allowed. What must remain genuinely unknowable: whether particular early rulings genuinely originate with the Prophet or his immediate community, as the tradition holds, or were later doctrine projected backward, as Schacht argued, cannot in most individual cases be settled by available evidence, and should be presented as an open historical question rather than adjudicated.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Mercy of Disagreement
comparativeNarrative.content: From inside the classical Sunni tradition, the plurality of the schools is not a weakness but a value. Sharia is God's perfect path, yet human jurists can only approximate it, so the tradition expected sincere scholars to differ. A famous saying held that their disagreement was a mercy, because it widened the room for sound practice across different lands and lives. On this view, the four surviving Sunni schools, with their distinct methods, are all valid routes toward the same goal. A careful jurist is rewarded even when he gets it wrong, because the honest effort is what God asks. This vantage shows that the tradition built its own fallibility into its base. It never imagined the single fixed code that both Islamists and Western critics assume.

comparativeNarrative.name: Reopening the Reasoning
comparativeNarrative.content: Reform-minded Muslim scholars look at the same tradition and stress its room for change. Because actual rulings are human readings of the sources, they can be reasoned again when times shift. These scholars revive ijtihad, the independent reasoning of a qualified jurist, and point to the law's deeper aims, such as protecting life and securing justice. From there they argue that old rulings on women, finance, and punishment can be responsibly rethought. This view shows that reform need not mean importing secular values from outside. It can mean using the tradition's own tools, the ones classical jurists used, to reach answers those jurists did not. The hard question it must face is where disciplined reinterpretation ends and wishful thinking begins, a worry traditionalists press hard.

comparativeNarrative.perspective: Critics of the modern Islamic state
comparativeNarrative.name: The State Swallows the Law
comparativeNarrative.content: A third vantage focuses on what the modern state did to the law. For most of its history, fiqh was the work of independent scholars, who held authority because they stood somewhat apart from rulers. When a modern government writes its chosen rulings into national statute and enforces them as 'the sharia,' it claims for the state an authority that once belonged to a plural community of jurists. Critics argue that this changes the thing itself. Probable human interpretation hardens into fixed command, and the gap between God's law and human understanding, which the tradition guarded, quietly closes. This view shows that many demands to 'implement sharia' are not a return to tradition at all. They are a modern political project dressed in classical words.

comparativeSummary: The same word, sharia, sits before three sets of readers who do not agree on what it even is. To the classical Sunni jurists, it is a divine ideal that humans can only approximate, so the disagreement of the schools is a mercy rather than a scandal. To modern reformers, that same human, plural character is a license: rulings reasoned by people can be reasoned again. To critics of the modern Islamic state, the danger is the opposite, a state that freezes one human interpretation into fixed law and stamps it with God's name. The deepest disagreement is not over any single ruling. It is over whether sharia is an aspiration, a method, or a code, and over who, after the Prophet, holds the authority to say what God requires.

rabbitHole.label: Islam
rabbitHole.reason: Sharia is the articulation of the way of life that the faith of Islam demands; understanding the religion's core commitments is prerequisite to understanding what its law is trying to express.

rabbitHole.label: The Quran
rabbitHole.reason: The Quran is the first and primary source of the law; every school begins its reasoning from the text Muslims hold to be God's own words.

rabbitHole.label: The Hadith
rabbitHole.reason: The hadith transmit the sunna, the Prophet's example that is the second source of law; the grade assigned to a report bears directly on whether it can ground a legal ruling, making hadith criticism inseparable from jurisprudence.

rabbitHole.label: Muhammad
rabbitHole.reason: The Prophet's life and rulings anchor the sunna from which the law is derived, and the closing of revelation at his death in 632 created the very problem of guidance that fiqh exists to solve.

rabbitHole.label: The Sunni-Shia Split
rabbitHole.reason: The divide over authority after the Prophet produced distinct bodies of law: the four Sunni schools and the Shia Jafari tradition, which grounds authority in transmission through the Prophet's family.

rabbitHole.label: The Ottoman Empire
rabbitHole.reason: The Ottomans paired sharia with sultanic kanun law and later codified Islamic civil rules, a major case of how a powerful state combined and reshaped Islamic law on the road to modern codification.

rabbitHole.label: The Millet System
rabbitHole.reason: The millet arrangement shows how a sharia-governed empire ruled its non-Muslim subjects under their own religious law, illustrating that classical Islamic governance did not apply fiqh uniformly to everyone.

rabbitHole.label: The Delhi Sultanate
rabbitHole.reason: The Delhi Sultanate applied Islamic law in a vast non-Muslim-majority land, a key case of how fiqh was adapted, enforced, and limited where most subjects were not Muslim.

reference.author: Hallaq, Wael B.
reference.title: An Introduction to Islamic Law
reference.annotation: The single best concise scholarly introduction to what Islamic law is, how its sources and schools work, and how the modern state transformed it; the entry's account of the sharia-fiqh distinction and the modern rupture leans heavily on Hallaq's framing.

reference.author: Hallaq, Wael B.
reference.title: The Impossible State: Islam, Politics, and Modernity's Moral Predicament
reference.annotation: The source of the entry's strong thesis that the modern centralizing nation-state is structurally incompatible with classical sharia; a distinct and more polemical work from Hallaq's introduction, engaged directly in the advanced and research layers.

reference.author: Schacht, Joseph
reference.title: The Origins of Muhammadan Jurisprudence
reference.annotation: The classic statement of the skeptical thesis on the law's formation, that much early legal material was retrospectively attributed to the Prophet; the entry presents it as the position later scholarship was developed to test and complicate.

reference.author: Kamali, Mohammad Hashim
reference.title: Principles of Islamic Jurisprudence
reference.annotation: The standard English-language exposition of usul al-fiqh from within the discipline, covering the Quran, sunna, ijma, qiyas, ijtihad, and maslaha; the entry relies on it to represent the source theory on the tradition's own terms rather than only through outside critique.

reference.author: Feldman, Noah
reference.title: The Fall and Rise of the Islamic State
reference.annotation: Traces how premodern Islamic governance balanced ruler and jurist and how that balance collapsed in the modern era; the entry uses it to complicate any simple before-and-after story about sharia and the state.

reference.author: Abou El Fadl, Khaled
reference.title: Speaking in God's Name: Islamic Law, Authority and Women
reference.annotation: A leading reformist jurist's analysis of how interpretive authority is claimed and abused in modern Islamic law; supports the entry's treatment of the reform debate and the danger of a human committee presenting its rulings as God's fixed law.

reference.author: Vikør, Knut S.
reference.title: Between God and the Sultan: A History of Islamic Law
reference.annotation: A clear narrative history of Islamic law from its origins through the schools to the modern period, useful for the entry's account of how the law formed, branched, and was reshaped by states; readable without specialist background.

commerce.title: An Introduction to Islamic Law
commerce.author: Wael B. Hallaq
commerce.note: The first book to open if you have never studied Islamic law. Hallaq explains the sharia-fiqh distinction, the sources, the schools, and the modern transformation in a short, lucid book a curious non-specialist can follow without any background.

commerce.title: Misquoting Muhammad: The Challenge and Choices of Interpreting the Prophet's Legacy
commerce.author: Jonathan A.C. Brown
commerce.note: Read this to feel how Islamic interpretation actually works from the inside. Brown shows scholars wrestling with texts and authority across the centuries; accessible, vivid, and the best correction to the idea that the law is a fixed rulebook.

commerce.title: Between God and the Sultan: A History of Islamic Law
commerce.author: Knut S. Vikør
commerce.note: For the reader ready to go past a first overview. Vikør narrates how the law formed, branched into schools, and met the modern state, with enough detail to see the moving parts but no assumed expertise.

commerce.title: Principles of Islamic Jurisprudence
commerce.author: Mohammad Hashim Kamali
commerce.note: For the reader who wants the source theory itself, in the tradition's own voice. Kamali lays out the Quran, sunna, consensus, analogy, and independent reasoning clearly; the place to learn how a ruling is actually derived.

commerce.title: The Impossible State: Islam, Politics, and Modernity's Moral Predicament
commerce.author: Wael B. Hallaq
commerce.note: For serious readers ready to argue. Hallaq's demanding claim that the modern state cannot house classical sharia is the most provocative recent thesis in the field; read it once you know the basics this entry lays out.

popularCulture.title: "Sharia law" as a Western scare phrase
popularCulture.description: In much Western media, the word sharia is a synonym for stoning and severed hands. The phrase "sharia law" gets used as if it named one harsh code creeping toward courthouses. This picture takes the rarest corner of a huge tradition and paints it as the whole, while most of the law is really about prayer, fasting, charity, and family, and has always come in many versions.

popularCulture.title: Halal and haram in everyday life
popularCulture.description: Many people first meet Islamic law through food labels and the words halal and haram, meaning allowed and forbidden. Halal meat, halal travel, even halal finance now fill shops and ads. This shows the law living quietly in daily choices, far from any court. But the simple two-way split flattens a richer system, which actually sorts acts across five steps from required down to forbidden, with most of life in the gentle middle.

popularCulture.title: Islamic banking and the ban on interest
popularCulture.description: A whole global industry markets itself as sharia-compliant banking, built around the old ban on riba, usually read as interest. Customers are offered mortgages and accounts that share risk instead of charging interest. This carries a real piece of the law into modern money. Critics, including some Muslim scholars, argue that many products just dress up ordinary interest in new names, which shows how hard old rules are to map onto new finance.

popularCulture.title: Anti-sharia laws in the United States
popularCulture.description: Starting around 2010, lawmakers in several US states pushed bills to ban courts from using sharia. The campaigns warned of a foreign law taking over, though no such takeover was happening. The episode shows how the word became a political weapon detached from what the law is. It treated private religious guidance, used by Muslims for prayer and family, as if it were an enemy legal code at the gates.

popularCulture.title: The Stoning of Soraya M. (2008)
popularCulture.description: This film dramatizes the real stoning of an Iranian woman falsely accused of adultery. It is wrenching, and it shows how such a punishment can be abused to destroy an innocent person. But by making the punishment stand for Islamic law as a whole, it feeds the very caricature it draws on. Classical jurists fenced this penalty with near-impossible proof rules meant to make it almost never apply.
